Applying Lasso/Ridge Regression Upon NFL Data

Predicting Team Wins Based On QB Play

Author

Jonathan Sears, Jasmine Cole, Noah Miller

Published

July 12, 2026

Slides: slides.html

Introduction

Under the stadium lights, the smell of popcorn and hot dogs and the band playing the “Fight Song” as one final play runs the last 3 seconds of the 4th quarter, that is the aesthetic of an American Football game. Fans come together to support, cheer and hope for their team’s victory. But what if fans or people in general had the ability to go into a match where they could predict the outcome? Being able to predict outcomes in the National Football League (NFL) through mult-dimensional data requires many different features and data such as home field advantage, player performance, team statistics, and game conditions which can cause multicollinearity, being a negative impact on the least square estimator in regression due to correlation between variables (McDonald 2009). 

With the implementation of a machine learning regression model such as Lasso  (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator), and Ridge we can simplify the variables and focus only on the important aspects that help to accurately predict wins. Both methods solve a modified optimization problem of the form:

\[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left\{ \|\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2 + \lambda P(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \right\}\]

where \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) represents the estimated coefficient vector, \(\arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) represents the values of \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) that minimize the objective function, \(\mathbf{y}\) represents the observed response vector, \(\mathbf{X}\) represents the design matrix of predictor variables, \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) represents the regression coefficient vector, \(\|\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_2^2\) represents the residual sum of squares, \(\lambda\) represents the regularization parameter controlling the strength of the penalty, and \(P(\boldsymbol{\beta})\) represents the penalty function applied to the coefficients.

The difference between LASSO and Ridge regression comes from how their penalties are calculated mathematically. Lasso uses a shrinkage technique called L1 regularization, where it zeros out the least important variables, leaving only variables that are optimal for accurate prediction. Ridge regression uses a shrinkage technique called L2 regularization where it helps reduce multicollinearity without removing variables from the original set of independent variables. This helps with some of the distortion of the predictors. Another area where ridge differs from LASSO is that in the end and final model, ridge retains all variables . LASSO is a “step-up” from ridge allowing for key variable selection, where ridge can struggle with its limitations due to retention of all variables. This highlights that LASSO’s ability to produce a subset of “key-variables” from a high-dimensional dataset, makes it highly efficient.

Previous research examining LASSO regression has consistently demonstrated its usefulness in datasets containing large numbers of predictors and correlated variables. Across applications in credit risks awareness (Geraldo-Campos et al. 2022), genetics, and other predictive modeling fields, LASSO has been shown to improve model interpretability by shrinking insignificant coefficients toward zero while retaining the most influential predictors (Bhattacharyya 2025; Jana and Hemalatha 2021; Lockhart et al. 2014). Sports outcomes in LASSO include ability to identify home-field advantage as a key-variable by narrowing down recruitment and box-score metrics (South and Egros 2020). Similarly, LASSO was utilized to significantly reduce computational complexity that identified an optimal configuration of motion sensors (Laufer et al. 2023). Such reduces model complexity and can improve predictive performance compared to traditional regression methods (Alshqaq and Abuzaid 2023; Bhattacharyya 2025; Lockhart et al. 2014). Studies have also shown that regularization techniques such as Ridge regression can effectively address multicollinearity by reducing coefficient variance without removing predictors from the model. One limitation discussed within the literature is that highly correlated variables can influence LASSO’s variable selection process, potentially causing relevant predictors to be excluded (Lockhart et al. 2014). Overall, the findings suggest that regularized regression methods provide a useful framework for prediction problems involving numerous and potentially correlated variables, making them applicable to NFL team performance analysis.

It is important to note, however, that LASSO has experienced some limitations in its performance ability in variable selection specifically with highly correlated variables and complex non-linear interactions. For example, lasso regression was compared against XGBoost to determine which methodology proved to be the most accurate at predicting fare prices for online cab services, in which XGBoost outperformed LASSO because “LASSO failed to make the grouped selection as it only took one variable from a group and ignored the remaining variables,” (Tarun G and Sriramya 2026). Despite this specific limitation, across multiple studies LASSO proves to be consistent in its ability to minimize prediction error and utilize mathematical frameworks to isolate key critical variables.

The main goal of this report is to examine the impact of multicollinearity on predicting NFL team success and to examine whether regularized regression models are better than a linear regression model. This will be seen by comparing a standard linear regression, Ridge regression, and Lasso regression ability to predict the number of regular-season wins by an NFL team using that team’s quarterback’s performance statistics. Predictor variables include a quarterback’s season standard statistics, as well as expected points added (EPA), a metric that measures how many net points a quarterback adds to their team’s scoring potential on passing plays, compared to the league-average expectation for any given down, distance, and field position. This report will also aim to find which quarterback performance statistics are most influential in predicting team performance. Understanding the impact of a quarterback’s performance on a team’s success and the most influential quarterback performance statistics provide valuable information for NFL analysts, coaches, and teams trying to find players that contribute to team success and improve team performance.

Methods

Following base cleaning of the data, numerical values were scaled to a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1. The data were standardized based upon:

\[x_{ij}^{\text{scaled}} = \frac{x_{ij}-\mu_j}{\sigma_j}\]

where \(x_{ij}\) represents value of feature j for observation i, \(\mu_j\) represents mean of feature j, and \(\sigma_j\) represents standard deviation of feature j.

A standard linear regression is used as a baseline, calculated by:

\[y_i = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_{ij} + \epsilon_i\]

where \(y_i\) represents the observed response for observation i, \(\beta_0\) represents the intercept term, \(x_{ij}\) represents the value of predictor variable j for observation i, \(\beta_j\) represents the coefficient associated with predictor, \(j\) represents the index of the predictor variables, and \(p\) represents the total number of predictor variables, and \(\epsilon_i\) represents the random error term for observation i.

For the lasso and ridge regression methods, the regularization parameter (\(\lambda\)) was selected using a 10-fold cross validation, defined as:

\[\mathrm{CV}_{10} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{k=1}^{10} L_k\]

ensuring a practical 90/10 train-test split, a common approach for numerical datasets of less than 1000 observations. Within the function, the dataset is divided into ten equally sized subsets, where nine folds are used to train the model while the remaining fold is reserved for validation (testing). The process is repeated until each fold has served as the validation set exactly once, and the resulting validation errors are averaged to estimate the model’s predictive performance. By evaluating each candidate value of the regularization parameter (\(\lambda\)) across all ten folds, what is identified is the value that minimizes the cross-validated error. The use of 10-fold cross-validation provides a balance between computational efficiency and reliable model evaluation, allowing nearly all observations to contribute to both model training and validation throughout the procedure, without heightened risk of overfitting or underfitting.

The lasso regression equation upon the data was calculated by:

\[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i-\beta_0-\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_jx_{ij} \right)^2 + \lambda\sum_{j=1}^{p}|\beta_j| \right\}\]

where \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) represents the estimated coefficient vector, \(\lambda\) represents the regularization parameter, and \(\lambda\sum_{j=1}^{p}|\beta_j|\) representing the L1 penalty.

The ridge regression equation upon the data was calculated by:

\[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i-\beta_0-\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_jx_{ij} \right)^2 + \lambda\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2 \right\}\]

where \(\lambda\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2\) represents the L2 penalty.

To evaluate the predictive performance of the Lasso and Ridge regression models, RMSE, MAE, and R² were calculated using the cross-validated predictions. RMSE and MAE quantify the average prediction error, with RMSE placing greater emphasis on larger errors due to the squared error term. RMSE is represented by:

\[\mathrm{RMSE} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2 }\]

where n represents the number of observations, \(y_i\) represents the observed response value for observation i, \(\hat{y}_i\) represents the predicted response value for observation i, and \(y_i-\hat{y}_i\) represents the prediction error.

MAE provides a measure that is less sensitive to extreme observations, represented by:

\[\mathrm{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left|y_i-\hat{y}_i\right|\]

R², however, measures the proportion of variation in the response variabl explained by the model,represented by:

\[R^2 = 1 - \frac{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2 }{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i-\bar{y})^2 }\]

where \(\bar{y}\) represents the mean of the observed response values, \(\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2\) represents the residual sum of squares, and \(\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i-\bar{y})^2\) represents the total sum of squares.

Lower RMSE and MAE values, coupled with a higher R², indicate a model that more effectively balances prediction accuracy with the coefficient shrinkage introduced through regularization.

The R programming language was the primary software used in this analysis. Data for the analysis was sourced from the nflreadr package; a repository of NFL data easily accessible within the R environment. For data preprocessing, the Python programming language package pandas was used within the R environment, as well as the native dplyr package. The corrplot, car, and ggplot2 packages were used for the preliminary analysis and analysis of the final models. Computation and selection of the LASSO and Ridge-based models was completed through use of glmnet.

Analysis and Results

Data Description and Visualization

A study was conducted to determine how quarterback (QB) season statistics correlates with how many wins a team has in that season. To determine how QB statistics correlates with team wins in a season, the dataset was created using publicly available NFL data accessed through the R package nflreadr. Weekly quarterback player statistics and NFL game schedules were collected for the 2015 through 2024 regular seasons.

Quarterback performance data were obtained using the load_player_stats() function. The dataset was filtered to include only players whose position was quarterback (QB). Weekly statistics were then aggregated into season-level totals for each quarterback and team. The aggregated variables included passing yards, passing touchdowns, interceptions, completions, pass attempts, passing EPA (Expected Points Added), rushing yards, and rushing touchdowns. In addition, completion percentage was calculated by dividing total completions by total pass attempts and multiplying by 100.

Team performance data were collected using the load_schedules() function. Only regular-season games were included by filtering for game_type == "REG". Wins were calculated separately for home and away teams by comparing the final scores of each game. Home and away wins were then combined to compute each team’s total number of regular-season victories for every season from 2015 through 2024.

The team win totals were merged with the quarterback season statistics using season and team as matching variables. Finally, quarterbacks with fewer than 200 pass attempts in a season were removed to ensure that the analysis included only quarterbacks with substantial playing time, reducing the influence of backup quarterbacks and small sample sizes.

The final dataset contains one observation for each qualifying quarterback-season and includes both individual performance statistics and the corresponding team’s regular-season win total. The description of variables are listed below in the chart.

Code 
# loading packages 
library(corrplot)
library(nflreadr)
library(dplyr)
Code 
# Load Data

years = 2015:2024
weekly = load_player_stats(years)
qbs <- weekly %>%
  filter(position == "QB")
qb_season <- qbs %>%
  group_by(player_display_name, season, team) %>%
  summarize(
    passing_yards = sum(passing_yards, na.rm = TRUE),
    passing_tds = sum(passing_tds, na.rm = TRUE),
    interceptions = sum(passing_interceptions, na.rm = TRUE),
    completions = sum(completions, na.rm = TRUE),
    attempts = sum(attempts, na.rm = TRUE),
    passing_epa = sum(passing_epa, na.rm = TRUE),
    rushing_yards = sum(rushing_yards, na.rm = TRUE),
    rushing_tds = sum(rushing_tds, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

qb_season <- qb_season %>%
  mutate(
    completion_pct = (completions / attempts) * 100
  )

write.csv(qb_season, "qb_season_stats.csv", row.names = FALSE)

schedules <- load_schedules(2015:2024)
team_wins <- schedules %>%
  filter(game_type == "REG") %>%
  mutate(
    home_win = ifelse(home_score > away_score, 1, 0),
    away_win = ifelse(away_score > home_score, 1, 0)
  )

home_wins <- team_wins %>%
  group_by(season, team = home_team) %>%
  summarize(wins = sum(home_win, na.rm = TRUE))

away_wins <- team_wins %>%
  group_by(season, team = away_team) %>%
  summarize(wins = sum(away_win, na.rm = TRUE))

team_total_wins <- bind_rows(home_wins, away_wins) %>%
  group_by(season, team) %>%
  summarize(wins = sum(wins))

qb_season <- qb_season %>%
  left_join(team_total_wins,
            by = c("season", "team"))

write.csv(qb_season,
          "qb_season_dataset.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)

qb_season <- qb_season %>%
  filter(attempts >= 200)


write.csv(qb_season,
          "qb_season_dataset.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)

qb_season1 <- qb_season %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(-player_display_name, -season, -team)
Code 
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)

variable_table <- data.frame(
  Variable = c(
    "passing_yards",
    "passing_tds",
    "interceptions",
    "completions",
    "attempts",
    "passing_epa",
    "rushing_yards",
    "rushing_tds",
    "completion_pct",
    "wins"
  ),
  Description = c(
    "Total number of passing yards recorded by the quarterback during the season.",
    "Total number of passing touchdowns thrown by the quarterback during the season.",
    "Total number of interceptions thrown by the quarterback during the season.",
    "Total number of completed passes by the quarterback during the season.",
    "Total number of pass attempts by the quarterback during the season.",
    "Total Expected Points Added (EPA) generated through passing plays during the season.",
    "Total number of rushing yards gained by the quarterback during the season.",
    "Total number of rushing touchdowns scored by the quarterback during the season.",
    "Completion percentage, calculated as (completions ÷ attempts) × 100.",
    "Total number of regular-season wins recorded by the quarterback's team (response variable)."
  ),
  Type = c(
    "Predictor",
    "Predictor",
    "Predictor",
    "Predictor",
    "Predictor",
    "Predictor",
    "Predictor",
    "Predictor",
    "Predictor",
    "Response"
  )
)

kable(variable_table,
      caption = "Table 1: Description of Variables Used in the Analysis")
Table 1: Description of Variables Used in the Analysis
Variable Description Type
passing_yards Total number of passing yards recorded by the quarterback during the season. Predictor
passing_tds Total number of passing touchdowns thrown by the quarterback during the season. Predictor
interceptions Total number of interceptions thrown by the quarterback during the season. Predictor
completions Total number of completed passes by the quarterback during the season. Predictor
attempts Total number of pass attempts by the quarterback during the season. Predictor
passing_epa Total Expected Points Added (EPA) generated through passing plays during the season. Predictor
rushing_yards Total number of rushing yards gained by the quarterback during the season. Predictor
rushing_tds Total number of rushing touchdowns scored by the quarterback during the season. Predictor
completion_pct Completion percentage, calculated as (completions ÷ attempts) × 100. Predictor
wins Total number of regular-season wins recorded by the quarterback’s team (response variable). Response

Figure 1: Correlation Plot between Variables

Code 
correlation_matrix = round(cor(qb_season1, use = "complete.obs"), 2)
corrplot(correlation_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper",
         tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 45)

According to the correlation plot above, the passing EPA has the strongest correlation with team wins (r = 0.66). Completion percentage showed a moderate correlation with wins (r=0.41). The rushing statistics like rushing touchdowns and rushing yards appeared to have the lowest positive relationship with wins (average r= 0.22) while interceptions had almost no linear relationship with wins (r=0.06). 

The analysis also identified substantial multicollinearity among several passing statistics. Completions and attempts were nearly perfectly correlated (r = 0.99), while passing yards showed very strong correlations with completions (r = 0.97) and attempts (r = 0.96). These findings suggest that some passing variables may provide redundant information and should be carefully considered during model development. 

Code 
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(qb_season1, aes(x = passing_tds, y = wins)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.7) + geom_smooth(method = "lm",color = "red") + labs(x="Passing touchdowns", y="Team wins", title = "Figure 2: Passing Touchdowns vs Team Wins")
Code 
ggplot(qb_season1, aes(x = passing_yards, y = wins)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.7) + geom_smooth(method = "lm") + labs(x="Passing yards", y="Team wins", title = "Figure 3: Passing Yards vs Team Wins")

Both scatterplots show that passing yards and passing touchdowns have a relative strong positive correlation to team wins. These scatterplots show that both variables, passing yards and passing touchdowns have a positive effect on team wins. The passing touchdowns versus team wins scatterplot shows a stronger relationship with less variability and spread in the data comparatively to passing yards versus team wins. The conclusion can be made that as passing touchdowns increase, team wins also increase and similarly as passing yards increase, team wins increase. This conclusion is beneficial in highlightly possible key variables that strongly influence team wins. 

Code 
VIF_Visualization_Model <- lm(wins ~ passing_yards + passing_tds + interceptions + completions + attempts + passing_epa + rushing_yards + rushing_tds + completion_pct, data = qb_season1)
library(car)
VIF_Visualization_Values <- vif(VIF_Visualization_Model)
VIF_Visualization_Values
 passing_yards    passing_tds  interceptions    completions       attempts 
     45.086638       7.620450       1.795463     378.630209     340.548019 
   passing_epa  rushing_yards    rushing_tds completion_pct 
      7.333929       2.044880       1.956828      10.734847
Code 
VIF_Visualization_Dataframe <- data.frame(Variable = names(VIF_Visualization_Values), VIF = as.numeric(VIF_Visualization_Values))
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(VIF_Visualization_Dataframe, aes(x = reorder(Variable, VIF), y = VIF)) + geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + geom_hline(yintercept = 5, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") + coord_flip() + labs(title = "Figure 4: Variance Inflation Factors (VIFs) of Dataset Predictors", x = "Predictor", y = "VIF") + theme_minimal()

Variance Inflation Factors (VIFs) of the predictors against the response variable (wins) are modeled based on a simple linear regression created for the purposes of this visualization. To combat inflated variances due to predictor multicollinearity, a VIF threshold of 5 was used, exhibited by the red-dashed line running along the y-axis. Predictors such as interceptions, rushing_tds, and rushing_yards satisfy this threshold. Although completion_pct, passing_tds, and passing_epa do not meet the threshold, they are close enough to at least consider in a final model if other evidence supports their inclusion. Completions, attempts, and passing_yards, however, indicate such high variances that they should likely not be used when refining a model.

Statistical Modeling

After collecting the quarterback and team data, several preprocessing steps were performed to prepare the dataset for analysis.

First, weekly quarterback statistics were aggregated into season-level totals for each quarterback and team from the 2015–2024 NFL regular seasons. Team regular-season win totals were calculated separately from the schedule data by summing home and away victories and then merged with the quarterback statistics using the season and team as matching variables.

A new variable, completion percentage, was created by dividing total completions by total pass attempts and multiplying by 100. This provided an additional measure of passing efficiency beyond the raw completion and attempt totals.

To ensure that only quarterbacks with meaningful playing time were included, seasons in which a quarterback attempted fewer than 200 passes were removed from the dataset. This filtering step reduced the influence of backup quarterbacks and players with limited appearances whose statistics may not be representative of a full season.

Variables that were not used as predictors, including the quarterback’s name, season, and team, were removed before model development. The remaining dataset consisted of nine predictor variables: passing yards, passing touchdowns, interceptions, completions, pass attempts, passing EPA, rushing yards, rushing touchdowns, and completion percentage. The response variable was the team’s total number of regular-season wins.

Before fitting the predictive models, the predictor variables were standardized so that each feature had a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. Standardization places variables measured on different scales onto a common scale, improving model performance and ensuring that variables with larger numerical ranges do not disproportionately influence the results, particularly for regularization methods such as Ridge and LASSO regression.

Finally, the cleaned dataset was randomly partitioned into training and testing sets using an 90/10 split. The training set, containing 90% of the observations, was used to fit and tune the predictive models, while the remaining 10% served as an independent test set for evaluating model performance on unseen data.

Code 
# Removal of non-numeric variables.

drop_vars <- c("player_display_name", "season", "team")  
qb_season <- qb_season
qb_season <- qb_season[, !(names(qb_season) %in% drop_vars)]

# Scaling of numeric variables.

qb_scaled <- qb_season
predictor_cols <- setdiff(names(qb_scaled), "wins")

qb_scaled[predictor_cols] <- scale(qb_scaled[predictor_cols])

# Creation of train/test split.

set.seed(123)
train_index <- sample(seq_len(nrow(qb_scaled)), 0.8 * nrow(qb_scaled))

train <- qb_scaled[train_index, ]
test  <- qb_scaled[-train_index, ]

# Removal of missing values.

train <- na.omit(train)
test  <- na.omit(test)

# Simple regression model creation.

LinReg <- lm(wins ~ ., data = train)

# Matrix design in preparation for glmnet.

x_train <- model.matrix(wins ~ ., data = train)[, -1]
y_train <- train$wins
x_test_raw <- model.matrix(wins ~ ., data = test)[, -1]
x_test <- x_test_raw[, colnames(x_train), drop = FALSE]

# Ridge and Lasso creation including 10-fold cross-validation.

library(glmnet)
set.seed(123)
ridge_cv <- cv.glmnet(x_train, y_train, alpha = 0, nfolds = 10)
set.seed(123)
lasso_cv <- cv.glmnet(x_train, y_train, alpha = 1, nfolds = 10)

# Prediction utilizing the test set.

ols_pred   <- predict(LinReg, newdata = test)
ridge_pred <- as.vector(predict(ridge_cv, newx = x_test, s = "lambda.min"))
lasso_pred <- as.vector(predict(lasso_cv, newx = x_test, s = "lambda.min"))

# Calculation of metrics.

rmse <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean((y - yhat)^2))
mae  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat))
r2   <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)

# Evaluation based on sought-after metrics.

lin_coef <- coef(LinReg)

comparison <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(lin_coef),
  Linear = as.numeric(lin_coef),
  Ridge = as.numeric(coef(ridge_cv, s = "lambda.min")),
  Lasso = as.numeric(coef(lasso_cv, s = "lambda.min"))
)
kable(comparison, caption = "Table 2: Comparison of Linear, Ridge, and Lasso Regression Coefficients for Predicting Team Wins")
Table 2: Comparison of Linear, Ridge, and Lasso Regression Coefficients for Predicting Team Wins
Variable Linear Ridge Lasso
(Intercept) 8.0209812 8.0241518 8.0175644
passing_yards -1.4889309 0.1312569 0.0000000
passing_tds 0.7997268 0.5997696 0.6582458
interceptions -0.1726284 -0.2419714 0.0000000
completions 0.0647495 0.1986404 0.1340993
attempts 1.3702028 0.1706677 0.0000000
passing_epa 1.4768132 0.8753844 1.1726364
rushing_yards 0.0424953 0.0879158 0.0000000
rushing_tds 0.2801655 0.2412277 0.1608867
completion_pct 0.1923807 0.2222890 0.0689544

Table 2 shows that the linear regression model assigns larger coefficients to features with potential correlations, such as passing yards and attempts, which may indicate the presence of multicollinearity among predictors. The Ridge regression model reduces the magnitude of all coefficients while retaining all predictors in the model, helping to reduce the impact of multicollinearity. The Lasso regression model performs feature selection by shrinking the coefficients of passing yards, interceptions, attempts, and rushing yards to zero. This suggests that these variables provide less unique predictive information compared to the other quarterback statistics included in the model.

An unexpected result from the linear regression model was the negative coefficient for passing yards (-0.474). Although higher passing yard totals are generally expected to contribute positively to team success, this coefficient represents the relationship between passing yards and wins while holding the other quarterback statistics constant. Since passing yards are highly correlated with variables such as attempts and completions, multicollinearity may have influenced the coefficient estimate and resulted in a counterintuitive direction. The Ridge and Lasso models reduce this issue by shrinking coefficients and reducing the influence of correlated predictors.

Code 
results <- data.frame(Model = c("OLS", "Ridge", "Lasso")
,
RMSE  = c(rmse(test$wins, ols_pred), rmse(test$wins, ridge_pred), rmse(test$wins, lasso_pred))
,
MAE = c(mae(test$wins, ols_pred), mae(test$wins, ridge_pred), mae(test$wins, lasso_pred))
,
R2 = c(r2(test$wins, ols_pred), r2(test$wins, ridge_pred), r2(test$wins, lasso_pred)))

kable(results, caption = "Table 3: Comparison of Model Performance Metrics for OLS, Ridge, and Lasso Regression" )
Table 3: Comparison of Model Performance Metrics for OLS, Ridge, and Lasso Regression
Model RMSE MAE R2
OLS 2.072554 1.663602 0.5885157
Ridge 2.092935 1.685985 0.5803830
Lasso 2.100832 1.691730 0.5772101

Table 3 compares the performance of the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS), Ridge, and Lasso regression models using Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and R². The OLS model achieved the best overall performance with the lowest RMSE (0.660), lowest MAE (0.530), and highest R² (0.589). Ridge and Lasso regression produced slightly higher prediction errors and slightly lower explained variance. Although regularization did not improve predictive performance in this dataset, Ridge and Lasso helped reduce the impact of multicollinearity by shrinking coefficient estimates and, in the case of Lasso, performing feature selection.

Code 
predictions <- data.frame(
  Actual = test$wins,
  OLS = ols_pred,
  Ridge = ridge_pred,
  Lasso = lasso_pred
)

head(predictions)
  Actual       OLS     Ridge     Lasso
1      7  7.248782  6.856215  7.165333
2      8  7.678029  7.746120  7.632581
3     11  8.382850  8.627638  8.173125
4      7  6.428119  6.427203  6.363633
5     10 11.033006 10.432507 10.628464
6      8  4.900332  4.904065  5.594762
Code 
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)

predictions_long <- predictions %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(OLS, Ridge, Lasso),
    names_to = "Model",
    values_to = "Predicted"
  )

ggplot(predictions_long, aes(x = Actual, y = Predicted, color = Model)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_abline(
    slope = 1,
    intercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Actual vs Predicted Team Wins",
    x = "Actual Wins",
    y = "Predicted Wins"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Conclusion

References

Alshqaq, S. S., and A. H. Abuzaid. 2023. “An Efficient Method for Variable Selection Based on Diagnostic-Lasso Regression.” Symmetry 15 (12): 2155. https://doi.org/10.3390/sym15122155.

Geraldo-Campos, L. A., J. J. Soria, and T. Pando-Ezcurra. 2022. “Machine Learning for Credit Risk in the Reactive Peru Program: A Comparison of the Lasso and Ridge Regression Models.” Economies 10 (8): 1–21. https://doi.org/10.3390/economies10080188.
Jana, A., and S. Hemalatha. 2021. “Football Player Performance Analysis Using Particle Swarm Optimization and Player Value Calculation Using Regression.” Journal of Physics: Conference Series 1911 (1): 012011. https://doi.org/10.1088/1742-6596/1911/1/012011.
Laufer, B., P. D. Docherty, R. Murray, et al. 2023. “Sensor Selection for Tidal Volume Determination via Linear Regression: Impact of Lasso Versus Ridge Regression.” Sensors 23 (17): 7407. https://doi.org/10.3390/s23177407.
Lockhart, Richard, Jonathan Taylor, Ryan J. Tibshirani, and Robert Tibshirani. 2014. “A Significance Test for the Lasso.” The Annals of Statistics 42 (2): 413–68. https://doi.org/10.1214/13-AOS1175.
McDonald, Gary C. 2009. “Ridge Regression.” WIREs Computational Statistics 1 (1): 93–100. https://doi.org/10.1002/wics.14.
South, C., and E. Egros. 2020. “Forecasting College Football Game Outcomes Using Modern Modeling Techniques.” Journal of Sports Analytics 6 (1): 25–33.
Tarun G, Venkat Sai, and P. Sriramya. 2026. “Analyzing OLA Data for Precise Price Prediction Using XGBoost Technique Comparing with Lasso Regression.” In Advances in Parallel Computing Algorithms, Tools and Paradigms. SAGE Publications. https://doi.org/10.3233/APC220050.
